coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of acriflavine-based carbon dots for adenine detection and a theoretical study on the detection mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Juan
Li, Li
Miao, Peng
Wang, Zheng
Chang, Zhimin
Shao, Dan
Shao, Haohua
Mei, Qian
Luo, Shi-zhong
Dong, Wen-Fei
coredata.dc:description: Adenine detection is important in biomedicine. Here, we report acriflavine-based carbon dots prepared via a one-step synthesis that can detect adenine in aqueous solution via fluorescence. Titration experiments show that the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots increases by up to 35% as the concentration of adenine increases. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy images show that carbon dots have a recognizable lattice structure. Based on this, we built a theoretical model using the DFT method to explain the mechanism by which adenine enhances fluorescence on the carbon dots.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adenine detection in aqueous solution with CDs
Work curve and application of CDs in real samples
Theoretical explanation on fluorescence enhancement of CDs by adenine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the CDs. Inset shows a HRTEM image of an individual as-prepared CD.
(a) XPS full survey spectrum of CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d).
UV–vis absorption (black line), PL excitation (blue line), and PL emission (red line) spectra of an aqueous dispersion of CDs.
(a) Changing emission spectra of CDs with excitation wavelength; (b) Influence of pH on the CD fluorescence intensity.
Changing fluorescence intensity of CDs as a function of adenine concentration.
Impact of other molecules on CD fluorescence intensity.

(a) Molecular structure of adenine, cytosine, thymine, and uracil. (b) Simulation of interactions between adenine and acriflavine. (c) Simulation of interactions between adenine and single-layer graphite flake (top view). (d) Simulation of interaction between adenine and single-layer graphite flake (side view).
Abridged general view by which adenine enhances the fluorescence of CDs.
Preparation of CDs and abridged general view of adenine detection with CDs.
Analytical results for the determination of adenine in samples.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of acriflavine-based carbon dots for adenine detection and a theoretical study on the detection mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), 88 Keling Road, Suzhou 215163, People's Republic of China

Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids
Center for Nano Science and Technology
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adenine detection is important in biomedicine. Here, we report acriflavine-based carbon dots prepared via a one-step synthesis that can detect adenine in aqueous solution via fluorescence. Titration experiments show that the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots increases by up to 35% as the concentration of adenine increases. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy images show that carbon dots have a recognizable lattice structure. Based on this, we built a theoretical model using the DFT method to explain the mechanism by which adenine enhances fluorescence on the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Purine derivatives are useful biomarkers of several diseases, including hyperuricosuria and arthritis, in clinical study [1–4]. Adenine is one of the main purine derivatives. It is a nucleic acid and an elementary unit of DNA and RNA. It participates in several vital activities, such as inheritance and metabolism [5–8]. Its metabolic defects can lead to many serious diseases, such as mental deficiency, cardiovascular disease, renal failure, arthritis, and toxemia [9,10]. Thus, quantitative determination of adenine is important to life science research. The current methods of adenine determination include chemiluminescent immunoassays [11,12], ultraviolet spectrophotometry [13], high-performance liquid chromatography [14,15], capillary electrophoresis [16], and electrochemical methods [17,18]. Although these methods provide excellent sensitivity and multi-element analysis, they are quite complex, expensive, time-consuming for sample preparation, and non-portable. Fluorescence is considered to be one of the most promising methods for adenine detection due to its inherent sensitivity, high selectivity and ease operation [19,20].

Quantum dots (QDs) have recently been used widely for fluorescence detection. Versus traditional fluorescent dyes, QDs offer narrow and symmetrical emission, adjustable emission color, unique optical and electronic properties, and high quantum yield. However, their poor stability and high toxicity markedly limit their broad applications. Carbon dots (CDs) are new carbon-based nanomaterials—discrete spherical particles less than 10 nm in size [21]. CDs have better performance than QDs and have excellent properties, such as chemical inertness, excellent optical stability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, good water solubility, low cost, and simple synthesis.

Wang et al. developed a sensitive and selective photoluminescence (PL) quenching assay for the detection of acetylcholine that uses reduced graphene oxide decorated with CDs [22]. He et al. reported CD-based fluorescent probe for “off-on” sensing of Hg2+ and I− [23]. Gao et al. prepared the CDs with red emission for sensing of Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+ in vitro and in vivo [24]. Singh et al. modified N-doped CDs with QPRTase as a fluorescent bioprobe for selective detection of the neurotoxin quinolinic acid in human serum [25].
This work uses a fluorescence enhancement method [26–28] to detect adenine. The CDs were synthesized with a one-step technique using a hydrothermal method with acriflavine as the raw material.

The CD fluorescence significantly enhanced when adenine was added, indicating that the CDs could measure adenine in the solution. Importantly, the presence of other ions almost had no interference with the detection of adenine.

Mechanistic studies include Qu et al., who found enhanced orange emission via surface charge engineering and surface metal cation functionalization. This increased carboxylate ions on the inner surface of the CDs, leading to an electron-rich inner surface [29]. Zhu et al. investigated the dynamic and PL center destruction quenching behaviors of CDs and then exploited the quenching behavior for various applications [30].

Here, we studied the fluorescence enhancement detection mechanism of CDs. We used theoretical calculations to simulate the interactions between adenine and single-layer CDs [31–34]. The adsorption of adenine on the CD surface enhances the rigidity of CDs, leading to enhanced fluorescence.

Acriflavine, adenine, cytosine, thymine, uracil, sucrose, monosodium glutamate, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2·2H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, Na2SO4, and ZnSO4·7H2O were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai) and were not further purified. Ultrapure water was used throughout.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEOL JEM-2100 (Japan) microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were collected with an EQUNOX 55 instrument using a diffuse reflectance scanning disc technique from 4000 to 400 cm−1 at room temperature. Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–vis spectroscopy. This was performed with a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) at room temperature. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded with an AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer.

Here, 20 μL adenine solution (1 mM) was added to 2 mL of 0.01 mg/mL CD in a cuvette. The relative fluorescence intensity was measured with an excitation wavelength of 460 nm and an emission of 516 nm. The impact of competing species (such as cytosine, thymine, uracil, sucrose, monosodium glutamate, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, Na2SO4, and ZnSO4) was measured similarly.

All of the calculations were performed according to the density functional theory (DFT) [35,36] on the Gaussian09 program [37]. The ground-state structures were optimized at B3LYP-D3/6-31G(d) level without any symmetry constraint.
This paper proposes a method to measure adenine concentration via CDs and fluorescence enhancement. The preparation and detection of the CDs are shown in Scheme 1 .

The CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method [38–40]. The acriflavine (0.6 g) was dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water and reacted at 200 °C for 12 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After the solution was cooled to room temperature, solid impurities were removed with centrifugation. The product was filtered with a 0.22-μm membrane filter three times. This was then transferred into a dialysis tube (MW = 1000) and dialyzed for 48 h. Finally, the retained fluid was then freeze-dried to a brown solid powder for further use.

The size and surface morphologies of CDs were studied with TEM. The size of the CDs is about 5 nm (Fig. 1 ). A further high-resolution image shows that most particles have a recognizable lattice structure and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which concurs with the faces of the graphite structure (100). These data indicate that the CDs have a layered structure similar to sp2 graphite flake. Meanwhile, some particles have a highly recognizable lattice fringe that complies with the results reported previously [24,41].

The functional group on the surface of CDs is represented via FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 ). The FT-IR spectrum of CD shows absorption bands at 3327 cm−1 and 3206 cm−1, corresponding to stretching vibrations at NH and of CH, respectively. The absorption peak at 1600 cm−1 is from CO stretching vibration. The FT-IR spectrum indicates there are amino and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CDs.

XPS measurements confirmed the composition and structure of the prepared CDs. Fig. 3a shows the full XPS spectra of CDs with peaks at 284.5 eV, 398 eV, and 531 eV, corresponding to C(1s), N (1s), and O(1s), respectively. The high-resolution spectra (Fig. 3b) of C1s consists of three peaks: 284.4 eV, 285.1 eV, and 288.2 eV. These are attributed to three carbon bonds (CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO, respectively). The high-resolution spectra (Fig. 3c) of N1s has peaks at 398.4 eV and 399.5 eV corresponding to N in a graphite-like structure (NC) and a NH bond from the NH2 group respectively. The two high resolution spectra peaks of O1s (Fig. 3d) are at 531.5 eV and 533.1 eV from CO and COH, respectively. The result of XPS data concurs with that of FT-IR data.

The optical properties of CDs were also studied. Fig. 4 shows that the absorption spectra of CDs covers the wavelength block from 250 nm to 600 nm. There is an obvious absorption peak at 460 nm. The fluorescence spectra show that the CDs have a symmetrical excitation peak and emission peak at 460 nm and 516 nm, respectively.

Fig. 5(a) shows the location and intensity of fluorescence emission peak of CDs as a function of excitation wavelength. The emission peak remains near 516 nm (with increasing intensity) as the excitation moves from 420 nm to 470 nm. Thus, the optimum excitation wavelength is 470 nm, and 460 nm was used from all of the subsequent experiments.

The effects of pH on the fluorescence stability of CDs were also investigated. Fig. 5(b) reflects the stability towards extreme pH (from 4 to 10). This shows that the synthesized CDs have very good fluorescence stability under different pH conditions. Due to its excellent stability, the as-prepared CD can be further explored in sensing and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adenine detection in aqueous solution with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were used to detect adenine in an aqueous solution. Adenine was added to 0.01 mg/mL carbon dots to a working concentration of 0.1 mM. Fig. 6 shows that the fluorescence emission of CDs at 516 nm increases by about 20%, and the fluorescence emission of CDs constantly strengthens as adenine concentration increases (by 0.01 mM every time). The amplitude of fluorescence enhancement significantly drops once the adenine concentration reaches 0.07 mM. The fluorescence intensity stops strengthening with increasing adenine concentration if the adenine concentration reaches 0.1 mM. In this case, the final amplification of fluorescence intensity is about 35% relative to the initial carbon dot solution.

We tested the influence of other molecules, including cytosine, thymine, uracil, sucrose, monosodium glutamate, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, Na2SO4, and ZnSO4, on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs similar to adenine detection. Fig. 7 shows the relative variation of fluorescence intensity of CDs with a 0.01 mg/mL CD solution and 0.1 mM interferents. The adenine enhancement is most significant (35%); cytosine, thymine, and uracil on fluorescence emission intensity of CDs are second most significant (10%). Sucrose is least significant (5% increase). Monosodium glutamate slightly weakens the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The presence of NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, Na2SO4, and ZnSO4 has little impact. Thus, CDs can detect adenine in solution via fluorescence enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Work curve and application of CDs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Moreover, through conducting a series similar experiment at different concentration of adenine (from 0.05 mg/L to 0.5 mg/L), a calibration curve was manufactured under a lower CD concentration (at 2 mg/L) and the results were simulated in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8 shows a good linear dependence in the range from 0.1 mg/L to 0.4 mg/L, and the linear regression equation was expressed as ΔF = 246.071 × C + 71.767 (that ΔF = F − F
0 , F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs at 516 nm in the absence and presence of adenines, respectively), with correlation coefficient of 0.991. The limit of detection (LOD), determined using the equation 3σ/S (that σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements of 6 replicates and S is the slope of the calibration curve) is 5.57 × 10−5 g/L.

The application of as-prepared CDs was investigated by detecting adenine in real samples with standard addition method. 10 tablets of vitamin B4 (also called adenine, from Tianjin Lisheng Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd.) were ground, dissolved, and configured as a sample solution. Different amounts of adenine were added into the sample solution to test recovery rates, and the results were shown in Table 1 . It was observed that the recovery rates of adenine were about 97.3–98.7%, indicating the assay was credible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical explanation on fluorescence enhancement of CDs by adenine
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental characterization shows that adenine has a significant enhancement effect on CD fluorescence; cytosine, thymine, and uracil also have little acceleration effects.

This paper also explains the fluorescence enhancement mechanism via theoretical simulations. The HRTEM images show that the CDs are formed via a sheet plane structure similar to graphene stacks. Adenine, cytosine, thymine, and uracil also are rigid planar molecules and consist of two or one aromatic nuclei (Fig. 9a). If these particles are in solution, then these small planar molecules can attach to the surface of the CDs via π-π conjugation. This enhances the rigidity of CDs; CDs are not vulnerable to energy loss due to their molecular vibration or rotation in the excited state. The excited state energy is more likely to be released via fluorescent radiation that enhances the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

To verify the mechanism, we simulated the interaction (Fig. 9b) between adenine and acriflavine (the CDs are prepared with acriflavine only) in solution via DFT. The figure shows that adenine and acriflavine are small planar molecules that can attach together in parallel via π–π conjugation with a distance of 3.29 Å between the two planes. We also simulated the interactions between monolayer graphene (approximate to the surface of the CDs) and adenine. Fig. 9c and d shows a top view and a side view after structural optimization. The figures show that the graphene face and the adenine molecules indeed combine together in a face-to-face parallel way with a distance of about 3.27 Å. The distance between the graphene face and the adenine molecules is shorter than that between acriflavine and adenine indicating that the CDs more tightly combine with adenine.

Adenine is a 2-loop planar structure while cytosine, thymine, and uracil have a single-loop structure. Thus, the interaction between adenine and the CD surface is more significant, which explains the enhanced impact of adenine relative to cytosine, thymine, and uracil.

In summary, we propose a theoretical model by which adenine enhances CD fluorescence (Fig. 10 ). The stratified graphene surface structure of CDs is good for absorbing small planar molecules, such as adenine, and this process enhances the rigidity of CDs and reduces the energy loss caused by vibration and rotation in the excited state. This enhances the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs.

In summary, green fluorescent CDs were prepared with a one-step method to measure adenine concentration in solution without interference from other ions or small molecules. We also simulated fluorescence enhancement via theoretical calculation. The CDs are formed by planes similar to graphene stacks, and the surface layer can absorb adenine molecules via π–π interactions. This enhances the rigidity of CDs, reduces the energy loss caused by vibration or rotation in the excited state, and enhances the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The CDs have high in vitro and in vivo detection potential and can be used for biomedical research. Future research will apply this mechanism to practical analysis/detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant Nos. 2017YFF0108600, 2017YFC0211900, and 2016YFF0103800), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 81771982, 61535010, 81771929, 21803075, and 8160071152), Key Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. KFZD-SW-210), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BE2015601), and the Science and Technology Department of Suzhou City (Nos. SS201539 and ZXY201434).
